20 min read
Just now

--

The leftmost-prefix rule, the 57× DATE() penalty, and the wide-selectivity trap. Why your created_at index isn’t working.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Ali Shah Lakhani on UnsplashA developer notices that a Laravel dashboard query is slow: “show me all events from the last 6 months.” They check the database, see that created_at doesn't have an index, and add one. They re-run the query. The performance hasn't improved at all. They run EXPLAIN. The query plan shows type: ALL, key: NULL — the index they just added isn't being used. The developer's first reaction is that PostgreSQL/MySQL is broken, or that the index isn't really there, or that something is fundamentally wrong with their setup.
The index is there. The database isn’t broken. The query planner is making a deliberate choice not to use the index because, for a range that includes 50% of the table’s rows, a full table scan is actually faster than an index lookup followed by 50,000 random row fetches. The index works perfectly for queries that return a small percentage of rows; it gets abandoned for queries that return a large percentage. The developer added the right index for the wrong query — or maybe a useful index for some queries, but not the one they were trying to speed up.
This article walks through why created_at indexes fail to help wide-range queries — verified with EXPLAIN output and…
